query: link between BCL11B haploinsufficiency and syndromic neurodevelopmental delay

retrieved_docs.title: Jacobsen Syndrome (11q Terminal Deletion Syndrome) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Jacobsen syndrome is a contiguous gene deletion disorder caused by a partial deletion of the long arm of chromosome 11. The size of the deletion varies, typically ranging from 7 to 16 Mb. [3] [4] Deletions larger than 20 Mb are expected to be lethal, but some cases of large deletions have been found in mosaic form, presenting as a severe phenotype. [5] Terminal deletions of chromosome 11 occur in 2 ways—a de novo pure terminal deletion (85% of cases) or an unbalanced familial translocation (15% of cases). Chromosomal breakage typically occurs within or beyond subband 11q23.3, often extending to the telomere. This partial chromosomal deletion results in the loss of all genes located distal to the breakpoint, which typically includes genes CBL2 , FRA11B , TECTA , BSX , NRGN , FEZ1 , CHK1 , ETS1 , FLI1 , KCNJ1 , BARX2 , NFRKB , APLP2 , ADAMTS8 , ADAMTS15 , OPCML , JAM3 , and B3GAT1 . Subband 11q23.3 includes FRA11B , a gene with a fragile site prone to double-stranded breakage, which serves as a breakpoint in some patients with Jacobsen syndrome. Smaller interstitial deletions within the Jacobsen syndrome region can also cause the condition, even without a full terminal deletion. [6] [7] [8] Regardless of the mode of pathogenicity, chromosomal deletions in Jacobsen syndrome can have incomplete penetrance and an unpredictable genotype-to-phenotype correlation.

retrieved_docs.title: Rett Syndrome -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Typical (classic) RTT : The diagnostic criteria for a typical presentation require the presence of regression, in addition to the main criteria, and the exclusion criteria must be met (see Image . Rett Syndrome Types). This category is an X-linked neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by a period of regression, partial or complete loss of purposeful hand use, and acquired speech, resulting in impairment of expressive language, hand-wringing movements, impaired gait, and ambulation, accompanied by stereotyped hand movements. [26] [27] A stage of stabilization will often follow regression, and sometimes, improvement may occur. Not all individuals with typical RTT will have postnatal deceleration in head growth. More than 95% of typical RTT cases have a pathogenic variant in the MECP2 gene. However, a recent case of clinically diagnosed typical RTT syndrome in an individual who lacked a genetic diagnosis despite 20 years of investigation and multiple rounds of sequencing the MECP2 gene was documented. Additional genetic testing using next-generation sequencing revealed a partial insertion of the BCL11A gene within exon 4 of MECP2 , caused by a small deletion in MECP2 , which may result in a possible disruption of MeCP2 function due to a frameshift. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Kabuki Syndrome -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Developmental delays : Early intervention is crucial for addressing developmental and intellectual disabilities commonly associated with this syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Delay -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Except for specific syndromes that include developmental delay, the vast majority of developmental delay is idiopathic.  Although the exact underlying pathophysiology is unknown, several mechanisms have been proposed by epidemiologic studies that lead to some sort of developmental delay and/or disabilities. Since some forms of developmental delay may run in families, genes have been assumed to play a significant role in developmental delay.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Delay -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic: There is no known genetic substrate for developmental delay, per se.  However, developmental patterns are often familial, including late walking and talking.  Nonetheless, these developmental delays can also represent risks for syndromes or developmental disorders.  There is a considerable variation in the genetics of developmental disorders, ranging from copy number variants (CNV's), insertions, deletions, and duplications. While most are rare variants, some represent common variants. The most common, known genetic factor for ID is Fragile X syndrome, a trinucleotide repeat disorder (CGG) targeting the Fragile Mental Retardation 1(FMR1) gene located on X-chromosome. Fragile X appears to confer risk for ASD, as well. Imprinting can also be seen as is the case of Prader-Willi and Angelman syndrome, which vary with paternal (Prader-Willi) and maternal (Angelman) loss of function on chromosome 15q. Both developmental delay and physical phenotypes can be associated with other disorders in which there are extra chromosomes or pieces of chromosomes, for example, Down syndrome (trisomy 21), Edward syndrome(trisomy 18) & Patau syndrome (trisomy 13). [8] [9] [10] [11] Other X-linked disorders include Coffin-Lowry syndrome predominantly in males and Rett syndrome in females. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: DiGeorge Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: About 90% of DGS cases are a result of a deletion in chromosome 22, more specifically on the long arm (q) at the 11.2 locus (22q11.2). Most of these mutations arise de novo with no genetic abnormalities noted in the genome of the parents of children with DGS. [1] Researchers have identified over 90 different genes at this locus, some of which they have studied in mouse models. The most studied of these genes is T-box transcription factor 1 (TBX1), which correlates with severe defects in the development of the heart, thymus, and parathyroid glands of mouse models. TBX1 also correlates with neuromicrovascular anomalies, which may be responsible for the behavioral and developmental abnormalities seen in DGS. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Intellectual Disability -- Etiology -- Genetic
retrieved_docs.content: IQ alterations can also occur as part of a clinical picture in other genetic syndromes. In many cases, the intellectual deficit appears to be limited and can occur at different stages of neurodevelopment, also in terms of cognitive decline. For example, in chromosome 22q11.2 deletion syndrome, DiGeorge syndrome, or velocardiofacial syndrome, which is one of the most common multiple anomaly syndromes in humans, is usually described as a cognitive decline rather than an early onset intellectual disability. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Angelman Syndrome -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Early intervention, developmental evaluation, and therapies should be started since infancy, to improve and reach the maximum developmental capacity of the patient. A family meeting and follow-ups will help the parent to be actively involved in the management of the patient. Other commodities should be evaluated and treated by every subspecialty to prevent any further possible complications. [Level5]

retrieved_docs.title: Jacobsen Syndrome (11q Terminal Deletion Syndrome) -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Patients and families should be educated on the risks associated with cardiac disease, immunodeficiency, bleeding disorders, or gastrointestinal disease. They should also recognize the key signs and symptoms that indicate heart failure, infection, bowel obstruction, or prolonged bleeding. Parents and patients should be counseled regarding the need for surgical intervention for cardiac, gastrointestinal, or skeletal or skull abnormalities. Finally, parents and families should be counseled on the need for early evaluation and intervention for developmental and intellectual disability and the need for genetic counseling as patients reach reproductive age.

retrieved_docs.title: Angelman Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Angelman syndrome is a rare disorder caused by loss of function of the maternal UBE3A. It presents in childhood with psychomotor delay, absent speech, ataxia, and motor impairments. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of Angelman syndrome and the role of the interprofessional team in managing patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Corpus Callosum Agenesis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The affected patients are mostly asymptomatic. Most of the patients present within the first two years of life. The core syndrome of primary agenesis of corpus callosum consists of the following symptoms [11] : Reduction in the interhemispheric transfer of sensory-motor information Delay in cognitive processing and Decrease in the complex information analysis and unacquainted task performance: increased vulnerability to elevation in cognitive demands. Patients with agenesis of corpus callosum usually present with: Mental retardation (60%) Vision problems (33%) Delay in the development of speech (29%) Seizures (25%) Feeding problems (20%) Impaired hand-eye coordination and Socio-behavioral disorders like attention-deficit-hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) and psychosis Isolated agenesis of corpus callosum patients may have normal intelligence quotient but may have some cognitive deficits, which group under the "core syndrome," which includes [11] :

The mutation is associated with different clinical expressions of the syndrome. Of note, there is proof of mosaicism in up to 20% of individuals with typical CdLS features. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Angelman Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the pathophysiology of Angelman syndrome. Describe the challenging management of Angelman syndrome. Outline the indications for genetic testing for Angelman syndrome. Explain the prognosis of Angelman syndrome. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 4 -- Community 2811
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are collectively classified as تقييد النمو الجنيني, a condition denoting fetal growth restriction. This classification highlights the interrelated nature of these perinatal indicators, which together signal impaired intrauterine growth and may necessitate focused obstetric monitoring and intervention to improve neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized as a related group of adverse perinatal outcomes and are classified together as a distinct clinical category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1089
retrieved_docs.content: with or lead to infectious complications around birth. Overall, the knowledge network portrays neonatal disorders as an interconnected group of conditions that include traumatic cerebral injury, intracranial and neonatal hemorrhage, perinatal infections, metabolic diseases such as transient neonatal diabetes, neurologic problems like developmental delay and epilepsy, and extreme prematurity. Birth trauma is a key antecedent of intracranial bleeding, which is classified under neonatal hemorrhage, while perinatal infection serves both as a parent condition for unspecified perinatal disorders and as a member of the broader neonatal disorder set. Recognizing these relationships aids clinicians in comprehensive assessment, diagnosis, and management of newborns presenting with any of these interrelated pathologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 151

Together, these relationships illustrate how gestational duration, low birth weight, fetal growth restriction, and associated perinatal disorders are organized within medical classification systems, aiding clinicians in diagnosing and managing newborns with short gestation and related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 110
retrieved_docs.content: syndrome is recorded as a comorbid condition, underscoring the relevance of metabolic disturbances alongside perinatal and infectious disorders. Birth trauma is linked to intracerebral bleeding in the fetus or newborn, emphasizing the need for neurological assessment after traumatic deliveries. Head circumference measurement (حج القحف) is clinically associated with a positive tuberculin test, indicating that abnormal head size may raise suspicion for underlying mycobacterial infection or related immune response. Collectively, these multilingual classification hierarchies integrate perinatal growth restriction, respiratory and cardiovascular complications, neonatal infections, pharmacologic influences on early pregnancy loss, diabetes‑related comorbidities, and genetic mechanisms, thereby supporting comprehensive diagnosis, coding, and patient management across perinatal and neonatal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of a liveborn infant encompasses a range of perinatal classifications that are linked through gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth status. A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a specific child category of liveborn, indicating that infants born alive at this stage are included within the broader liveborn group. The duration of gestation has an inverse‑isa relationship with liveborn, reflecting that shorter gestational periods are associated with distinct clinical classifications of liveborn infants. One major classification of liveborn infants is short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation. This category both classifies liveborn and is self‑referential, emphasizing its central role in describing infants born with reduced gestational length, low birth weight, or impaired growth. The disorder relating to short gestation AND/OR low birthweight is positioned as a parent of liveborn, indicating that this disorder underlies the

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 633
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes (مرض السكري) is presented as a central disease that is linked to a wide spectrum of clinical conditions and genetic factors. Various forms of neonatal diabetes, including DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 1 and 2, as well as the syndrome described as Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, are all mapped to diabetes, indicating that early‑onset glucose dysregulation can coexist with neurodevelopmental abnormalities. Dysfunction of the human INSR protein is identified as a gene‑product malfunction that is associated with the development of diabetes, underscoring a molecular basis for the disorder. Clinically, diabetes is associated with several serious complications affecting multiple organ systems. Retinal disorder (اضطراب في الشبكية) and congestive heart failure (فشل قلبي احتقاني) are both noted as conditions that commonly co‑occur with diabetes, reflecting its impact on vascular and cardiac health. Metabolic syndrome (متلازمة الأيض) is listed as a child

Recognizing these interconnections, the associated syndromes, and the relevant laboratory parameters (e.g., total hemoglobin) is essential for accurate diagnosis, interpretation of test results, and targeted management of infants during the neonatal and early infancy periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1841
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, and Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes are all mapped to the disease مرض السكري, indicating that these specific neonatal forms of diabetes and the syndrome that combines developmental delay, epilepsy, and neonatal diabetes are considered manifestations of diabetes. مرض السكري is clinically associated with اضطراب في الشبكية, reflecting the well‑known link between diabetes and retinal disorders such as diabetic retinopathy. In addition, malfunction of the human INSR protein is gene‑product‑associated with مرض السكري, highlighting the role of insulin receptor defects in the pathogenesis of diabetes. Finally, متلازمة الأيض is described as a child condition of مرض السكري, underscoring the close relationship between diabetes and metabolic syndrome in patient care and disease management.

Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth trajectories define a network of classifications—from specific gestational milestones (29‑30 weeks) to broad perinatal disorder groups—providing a comprehensive framework for understanding, diagnosing, and managing health issues in liveborn infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 509
retrieved_docs.content: developmental delay, epilepsy, and neonatal diabetes within the same diagnostic framework as other neonatal disorders. Overall, the knowledge network connects perinatal insults—such as birth trauma, intracerebral hemorrhage, and foetal undernutrition—to a continuum of disorders ranging from metabolic (transient neonatal diabetes) to neurological (developmental delay, epilepsy, cerebral injury) conditions in infants and newborns. Recognizing these interrelationships is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and comprehensive patient care in the neonatal period.

Together, these relationships illustrate a hierarchical and classificatory framework in which perinatal conditions, specific respiratory/cardiovascular disorders, growth‑restriction syndromes, low‑birth‑weight classifications, and perinatal infections are interrelated, facilitating comprehensive assessment and coordinated care for infants affected by these overlapping medical issues.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1504
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder) and Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes are both mapped to the Arabic term اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, indicating that they are classified as neonatal disorders. This mapping underscores that transient neonatal diabetes mellitus and the syndrome combining developmental delay, epilepsy, and neonatal diabetes represent related early‑life metabolic and neurological conditions, highlighting the need for integrated recognition and management of such disorders in newborns.
